instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

text_blocks.text: What You C is What You Get

runs.text: What You C is What You Get

text_blocks.text: C is not a “very high-level” language, nor a “big” one, and is not specialized to any particular area of application. But its absence of restrictions and its generality make it more convenient and effective for many tasks than supposedly more powerful languages.

runs.text: C is not a “very high-level” language, nor a “big” one, and is not specialized to any particular area of application. But its absence of restrictions and its generality make it more convenient and effective for many tasks than supposedly more powerful languages.

text_blocks.text: Allows writing programs to exploit underlying features of the architecture – memory management, special instructions, parallelism.

runs.text: Allows writing programs to exploit underlying features of the architecture – memory management, special instructions, parallelism.

images.ai_description: The image shows the cover of "The C Programming Language," Second Edition, by Brian W. Kernighan and Dennis M. Ritchie, featuring ANSI C.

text_blocks.text: Python, Java, R, JS are all written in C.

runs.text: Python, Java, R, JS are all written in C.

images.ai_description: Programming language ranking table showing top ten languages by score. Python leads with 100.0, followed by Java, C, C++, and R. JavaScript ranks sixth.

text_blocks.text: TIOBE Programming Community index is an indicator of the popularity of programming languages

runs.text: TIOBE Programming Community index is an indicator of the popularity of programming languages

images.ai_description: Table ranking programming languages from January 2020 to January 2021, showing percentage ratings and changes. Notable increases for Python and C; Visual Basic decreased.

text_blocks.text: Code is generally converted into machine code.

runs.text: Code is generally converted into machine code.

text_blocks.text: Java, by contrast, indirectly converts to machine code using a byte-code.

runs.text: Java, by contrast, indirectly converts to machine code using a byte-code.

text_blocks.text: Python, by contrast to both, interprets the code.

runs.text: Python, by contrast to both, interprets the code.

text_blocks.text: The difference is in a trade-off about when and how to create a machine-level representation of the source code.

runs.text: The difference is in a trade-off about when and how to create a machine-level representation of the source code.

text_blocks.text: A general C compiler will typically convert *.c source files into an intermediate *.o object file. Then, it will link these together to form an executable.

runs.text: A general C compiler will typically convert *.c source files into an intermediate *.o object file. Then, it will
these together to form an executable.

text_blocks.text: Assembly is also part of this process, but it is done behind the scenes.

runs.text: Assembly is also part of this process, but it is done behind the scenes.

text_blocks.text: You can have gcc (a common C compiler) spit out the assembly if you want!

runs.text: (a common C compiler) spit out the assembly if you want!

text_blocks.text: The compiler takes source code (*.c files) and translates them into machine code.

runs.text: The compiler takes source code (*.c files) and translates them into machine code.

text_blocks.text: This file is called an “object file” and is just potentially one part of your overall project.

runs.text: ” and is just potentially one part of your overall project.

text_blocks.text: The machine code is not quite an executable.

runs.text: The machine code is not quite an executable.

text_blocks.text: This object file is JUST representing the code for that particular source file.

runs.text: This object file is JUST representing the code for that particular source file.

text_blocks.text: You may require extra stuff provided by the system elsewhere.

runs.text: You may require extra stuff provided by the system elsewhere.

text_blocks.text: For instance, one file may contain certain common functionality and then this is invoked by your program elsewhere.

runs.text: For instance, one file may contain certain common functionality and then this is invoked by your program elsewhere.

text_blocks.text: You break your project up into pieces similarly to your Java programs.

runs.text: You break your project up into pieces similarly to your Java programs.

text_blocks.text: Then, each piece is merged together to form the executable.

runs.text: Then, each piece is merged together to form the executable.

text_blocks.text: The name refers to how the references to functions, etc, between files are now filled in.

runs.text: The name refers to how the references to functions,
, between files are now filled in.

text_blocks.text: Before this step… it is unclear where functions will end up in the final executable.

runs.text: Before this step… it is unclear
functions will end up in the final executable.

text_blocks.text: Keep this in mind as we look at memory and pointers later!

runs.text: Keep this in mind as we look at memory and pointers later!

text_blocks.text: code goes in, sausage object files come out
Some compilers output assembly and rely on an assembler to produce machine code

runs.text: Some compilers output assembly and rely on an assembler to produce machine code

text_blocks.text: These days, it's common for the compiler itself to produce machine code, or some kind of platform-independent assembly code

runs.text: These days, it's common for the compiler itself to produce machine code, or some kind of platform-independent assembly code

images.ai_description: Illustration of a sausage link chain with evenly spaced, curved segments forming a wave pattern. Ideal for discussing food processing or design concepts.

text_blocks.text: Compiler + Linker translates code into machine code.

runs.text: Compiler + Linker translates code into machine code.

text_blocks.text: Machine code can be directly loaded by the OS and executed by the hardware. Fast!!

runs.text: Machine code can be directly loaded by the OS and executed by the hardware. Fast!!

text_blocks.text: New hardware targets require recompilation in order to execute on those new systems.

runs.text: New hardware targets require recompilation in order to execute on those new systems.

text_blocks.text: Interpreter is written in some language (e.g. C) that is itself translated into machine code.

runs.text: Interpreter is written in some language (e.g. C) that is itself translated into machine code.

text_blocks.text: The Python source code is then executed as it is read by the interpreter. Usually slower.

runs.text: The Python source code is then executed as it is read by the interpreter. Usually slower.

text_blocks.text: Very portable! No reliance on hardware beyond the interpreter.

runs.text: Very portable! No reliance on hardware beyond the interpreter.

text_blocks.text: Java translates source to a “byte code” which is a made-up architecture, but it resembles machine code somewhat.

runs.text: Java translates source to a “byte code” which is a made-up architecture, but it resembles machine code somewhat.

text_blocks.text: Technically, architectures could execute this byte code directly.
But these were never successful or practical.

runs.text: But these were never successful or practical.

text_blocks.text: Instead, a type of virtual machine simulates that pseudo-architecture. (interpretation)

runs.text: Instead, a type of virtual machine simulates that pseudo-architecture. (interpretation)

text_blocks.text: Periodically, the fake byte code is translated into machine code.

runs.text: Periodically, the fake byte code is translated into machine code.

text_blocks.text: This is a type of delayed compilation! Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation.

runs.text: This is a type of delayed compilation! Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation.

text_blocks.text: This is a compromise to either approach.

runs.text: This is a compromise to either approach.

text_blocks.text: I don’t think the JVM-style JIT is going away any time soon.

runs.text: I don’t think the JVM-style JIT is going away any time soon.

runs.text: // The rules for printing strings are much like Java.

text_blocks.text: You will see a lot of different styles of C in the world at large.

runs.text: You will see a lot of different styles of C in the world at large.

text_blocks.text: The syntax has changed very little.

runs.text: The syntax has changed very little.

text_blocks.text: There have been a few different standard revisions.

runs.text: There have been a few different standard revisions.

text_blocks.text: C99 – Adds ‘complex’ numbers and single-line comments

runs.text: C99 – Adds ‘complex’ numbers and single-line comments

text_blocks.text: C11 – Newer than 99 (laughs in Y2K bug) starts to standardize		Unicode and threading libraries.

runs.text: C11 – Newer than 99 (laughs in Y2K bug) starts to standardize

text_blocks.text: C18 – Minor refinement of C11. The current C standard.

runs.text: C18 – Minor refinement of C11. The current C standard.

text_blocks.text: We will more or less focus on the C99 standard in our course.

runs.text: We will more or less focus on the C99 standard in our course.

text_blocks.text: I’ll try to point out some newer things if they are relevant.

runs.text: I’ll try to point out some newer things if they are relevant.

text_blocks.text: Nothing can be said to be certain, except death and C-like syntaxes.

runs.text: Nothing can be said to be certain, except death and C-like syntaxes.

text_blocks.text: The C language is incredibly simplistic.

runs.text: The C language is incredibly simplistic.

text_blocks.text: To add some constrained complexity, there is a macro language.

runs.text: To add some constrained complexity, there is a macro language.

runs.text: This code does not get translated to machine code, but to more code!

input.notes: C: The actual size of integer types varies by implementation. The only guarantee is that the long long is not smaller than long, which is not smaller than int, which is not smaller than short. Also, int should be the integer type that the target processor is most efficient working with. This allows great flexibility: for example, all types can be 64-bit.
